sourceFiles.name: .fixed-models/vehicle-model__review-2026-03-25T10-03-11-487Z__selected-19-issues__2026-03-25T10-40-30-102Z.sysml

results.name: Completeness
results.rawText:
Model Characterisation
- System purpose: A vehicle model intended to represent a road vehicle, its internal structure, operating modes, external context, requirements, behaviours, and verification/analysis artifacts.
- Operational domain: Automotive / ground vehicle.
- Lifecycle phase: Preliminary design / early architecture and verification planning. The model includes structure, context, requirements, behaviours, constraints, and analyses, but many details are still abstract.
- Model scope: A mostly self-contained demonstrator-style system model centered on a single vehicle instance (`myCar`) plus context, selected use cases, and verification artifacts.
- Known scope limitations: The model appears intentionally illustrative rather than production-complete. There is no explicit statement excluding software, detailed energy/powertrain interfaces, or full requirement sets, so completeness checks for those areas are still applicable where the model claims coverage.

CHECK PASSED: 1. Requirements Coverage - The model contains multiple `requirement def` elements with documentation text and subjects, including functional and some non-functional/specification-style requirements.

ISSUE [COMPLETENESS-001]
Element:        VehicleModel::PerformanceReq
Check:          1. Requirements Coverage
Finding:        The requirement has documentation but no subject identifying the constrained system element and no linked `satisfy` or `verify` relationship from the requirement itself or an explicit pending/TBD marker.
Evidence:       `requirement def PerformanceReq { doc /* The vehicle shall accelerate from 0 to 60 mph in less than 6 seconds */ }`
Score:          0.68  -  A top-level performance requirement without subject/traceability can drive incomplete verification and weak design accountability.
Confidence:     HIGH
Impact:         Requirement allocation and verification coverage analysis cannot reliably determine what element is constrained or how compliance is demonstrated.
Recommendation: Add a `subject vehicle : Vehicle;` (or other intended subject) and link the requirement to satisfaction/verification elements using explicit `satisfy`/`verify` relationships or mark it pending.

ISSUE [COMPLETENESS-002]
Element:        VehicleModel::SafetyReq
Check:          1. Requirements Coverage
Finding:        The requirement lacks a subject identifying what part of the system it constrains and has no explicit linked satisfaction/verification relationship or pending/TBD status.
Evidence:       `requirement def SafetyReq { doc /* The vehicle shall have adequate braking performance */ }`
Score:          0.70  -  A safety-related requirement lacking subject and traceability is likely to cause verification gaps or incorrect assumptions.
Confidence:     HIGH
Impact:         Safety analysis and verification planning cannot unambiguously trace what design element must satisfy the braking requirement.
Recommendation: Add an explicit subject, refine the vague text into measurable terms, and create explicit `satisfy`/`verify` relationships to the relevant model and verification elements.

ISSUE [COMPLETENESS-003]
Element:        Model-Wide
Check:          1. Requirements Coverage
Finding:        No derived requirements or refinement links are present connecting high-level requirements to the more detailed specification requirements.
Evidence:       High-level requirements such as `PerformanceReq` and `SafetyReq` exist, and many detailed specification requirements exist, but there is no `derivedReq` or refinement relation anywhere in the model text.
Score:          0.52  -  Missing derivation weakens completeness and can lead to missing downstream requirements, though analysis is not fully blocked.
Confidence:     HIGH
Impact:         It is difficult to show that lower-level design/specification requirements fully elaborate stakeholder intent.
Recommendation: Add `derivedReq` or refinement relationships from stakeholder requirements to detailed mass, speed, power, braking, interface, and scenario requirements.

ISSUE [COMPLETENESS-004]
Element:        Model-Wide
Check:          1. Requirements Coverage
Finding:        Non-functional requirement coverage is incomplete; safety, maintainability, reliability, and interoperability are only partially represented and mostly not measurable.
Evidence:       Present requirements cover performance, safety, mass, speed, and interface completeness, but no explicit measurable reliability, maintainability, or interoperability requirements are defined.
Score:          0.55  -  Important non-functional omissions can lead to incomplete design decisions, especially outside nominal performance.
Confidence:     MEDIUM  -  This depends partly on whether the model intends to represent a full requirement baseline or only a selected subset.
Impact:         Trade studies and verification planning for lifecycle qualities may proceed without needed constraints.
Recommendation: Add measurable non-functional requirements for reliability, maintainability, and interoperability, or explicitly state these are out of scope.

CHECK PASSED: 2. System Boundary - The model defines a top-level `Vehicle`, external context elements (`Driver`, `RoadInfrastructure`, `Atmosphere`, `TrafficSystem`), and a context part with explicit boundary connections.

ISSUE [COMPLETENESS-005]
Element:        VehicleModel::myCar
Check:          3. Structural Decomposition
Finding:        The `wheels : Wheel[4]` parts are declared but never populated with required attribute values despite wheel-related requirements existing for diameter and width.
Evidence:       In `part def Vehicle`, `part wheels : Wheel[4];` is declared. In `part myCar : Vehicle`, there are specializations for `engine` and `transmission`, but none for `wheels`.
Score:          0.63  -  Missing leaf component values leaves physical analysis and requirement satisfaction incomplete for a key subsystem.
Confidence:     HIGH
Impact:         Wheel-related analyses, requirement checks, and interface reasoning cannot be completed for the instantiated vehicle.
Recommendation: Instantiate or specialize all four wheel parts in `myCar` and assign required attributes such as `diameter` and `width`.

ISSUE [COMPLETENESS-006]
Element:        VehicleModel::Wheel
Check:          3. Structural Decomposition
Finding:        The leaf-level component definition lacks enough attributes/interfaces to be usable in analysis and integration beyond geometry.
Evidence:       `part def Wheel { attribute diameter : Real; attribute width : Real; }`
Score:          0.41  -  The wheel is under-specified for many analyses, but the exact missing properties depend on intended analysis depth.
Confidence:     MEDIUM  -  Sufficiency depends on project phase and which analyses are intended beyond the included braking/load examples.
Impact:         Detailed mass, traction, rotational, and road-interface analyses cannot be supported reliably.
Recommendation: Add relevant attributes/interfaces for current lifecycle needs, such as mass, inertia, material/tire properties, and any wheel-road or axle interface if in scope.

ISSUE [COMPLETENESS-007]
Element:        VehicleModel::EnergyConsumer
Check:          3. Structural Decomposition
Finding:        A defined/instantiated element exists outside the main composition hierarchy without contextual role or integration, making it effectively an orphaned model fragment.
Evidence:       `part def EnergyConsumer { port powerIn : PowerPort; }` and `part externalLoad : EnergyConsumer;` appear, but `externalLoad` is not used in any composition, context, or connection.
Score:          0.30  -  This is mainly a completeness/consistency gap that weakens model usability more than it directly causes wrong results.
Confidence:     HIGH
Impact:         Readers cannot determine whether the external load is in scope, intentionally external, or a missing context/interface element.
Recommendation: Either integrate `externalLoad` into a context model with connections, or remove it / mark it explicitly as a placeholder.

ISSUE [COMPLETENESS-008]
Element:        VehicleModel::Vehicle::powerOut
Check:          4. Interface & Connection Completeness
Finding:        The boundary port is defined on the vehicle but is not connected to any internal source or external counterpart in any usage context.
Evidence:       `out port powerOut : PowerPort;` is declared on `Vehicle`, but no `connect` statement references `powerOut` anywhere in the model.
Score:          0.74  -  An unconnected boundary port likely causes interface analysis errors and incomplete context/integration understanding.
Confidence:     HIGH
Impact:         Boundary interface completeness claims cannot be validated, and power flow/integration analysis is incomplete.
Recommendation: Connect `powerOut` to an internal provider/consumer and to an external element in an appropriate context, or remove it if not part of the intended boundary.

ISSUE [COMPLETENESS-009]
Element:        Model-Wide
Check:          4. Interface & Connection Completeness
Finding:        Ports are typed but do not declare explicit directions for their contained flows/signals, and no separate flow typing is provided for the exchanged items/attributes.
Evidence:       Port definitions such as `port def ControlPort { attribute steeringCommand : Real; attribute throttleCommand : Real; attribute brakeCommand : Real; }` and similar definitions only contain attributes; they do not declare item/flow directions.
Score:          0.58  -  Missing explicit directional semantics can lead to ambiguous interface interpretation and incorrect connection reasoning.
Confidence:     HIGH
Impact:         Interface compatibility, flow analysis, and allocation of sender/receiver responsibilities are weakened.
Recommendation: Define directional features or explicit flow/item semantics for each port interface and align them with boundary/interface usage.

ISSUE [COMPLETENESS-010]
Element:        VehicleModel::DriveCarFlow
Check:          5. Behaviour Completeness
Finding:        The action uses `StartVehicle` and `StopVehicle` as subactions, but corresponding action definitions are not present in the model.
Evidence:       `action start : StartVehicle; ... action stop : StopVehicle;` appears in `action def DriveCarFlow`, but there is no `action def StartVehicle` or `action def StopVehicle` in the model text; only `use case def StartVehicle` and no `StopVehicle` definition at all.
Score:          0.88  -  Referencing undefined behavioural elements is likely to block execution/trace analysis of the scenario.
Confidence:     HIGH
Impact:         Behaviour elaboration for the drive flow is incomplete and cannot be consistently interpreted or reused.
Recommendation: Define `action def StartVehicle` and `action def StopVehicle`, or replace these subactions with existing behavioural elements of the correct kind.

ISSUE [COMPLETENESS-011]
Element:        VehicleModel::ProvidePropulsion; VehicleModel::TransferTorque; VehicleModel::GenerateRoadTraction
Check:          5. Behaviour Completeness
Finding:        Operationally significant actions are stubs with only documentation and no typed inputs, outputs, or internal body/decomposition.
Evidence:       Each action definition contains only `doc` text, e.g. `action def ProvidePropulsion { doc /* Generate propulsion power for vehicle motion */ }`
Score:          0.67  -  Key functional behaviours without interfaces or body leave functional analysis and allocation incomplete.
Confidence:     HIGH
Impact:         It is not possible to reason about data/control flow, decomposition, or mapping of these functions to structure.
Recommendation: Add typed inputs/outputs and either a body (`perform`, `succession`, flows) or explicitly mark them as leaf actions with sufficient interface detail.

ISSUE [COMPLETENESS-012]
Element:        VehicleModel::Vehicle::OperatingModes
Check:          5. Behaviour Completeness
Finding:        The state machine has no explicit triggers, guards, or effects on any transition.
Evidence:       Transitions are defined only as `transition OffToStarting first Off then Starting;` and similarly for all others, with no trigger/guard/effect clauses.
Score:          0.66  -  State progression without transition conditions is likely to mislead mode logic and verification reasoning.
Confidence:     HIGH
Impact:         Mode logic, fault handling, and scenario-to-state consistency cannot be validated with confidence.
Recommendation: Add triggers, guards, and effects where applicable to each transition, especially for startup, shutdown, degraded, and safe-state behavior.

ISSUE [COMPLETENESS-013]
Element:        VehicleModel::Vehicle::OperatingModes
Check:          5. Behaviour Completeness
Finding:        The state machine does not define an explicit initial pseudostate/initial transition.
Evidence:       The state definition lists states and transitions, but there is no initial state construct; the only hint is `transition OffToStarting first Off then Starting;`.
Score:          0.61  -  Missing initialization undermines reachability and execution semantics for the mode model.
Confidence:     HIGH
Impact:         Reachability analysis and scenario validation against operational modes are incomplete.
Recommendation: Add an explicit initial state/pseudostate leading to the intended starting state.

ISSUE [COMPLETENESS-014]
Element:        VehicleModel::Vehicle::OperatingModes
Check:          5. Behaviour Completeness
Finding:        Several states have no entry, exit, or `do` behaviour despite representing operationally meaningful modes.
Evidence:       States `Off`, `Starting`, `Idle`, `Driving`, `ShuttingDown`, `DegradedOperation`, and `SafeState` are declared without any internal behaviors.
Score:          0.39  -  This weakens behavioral completeness, though some projects may defer state internal behavior to later phases.
Confidence:     MEDIUM  -  Whether every state requires entry/exit/do behavior depends on intended level of detail at this phase.
Impact:         The model provides limited guidance on what the system does while in each mode.
Recommendation: Add entry/exit/do behaviors for states where significant actions occur, or document that the state machine is intentionally mode-only at this phase.

ISSUE [COMPLETENESS-015]
Element:        Model-Wide
Check:          6. Value & Constraint Completeness
Finding:        Numerical attributes throughout the model are typed as bare `Real` without explicit units or specialized value types.
Evidence:       Examples include `attribute mass : Real;`, `attribute power : Real;`, `attribute maxSpeed : Real;`, `attribute voltage : Real;`, `attribute ambientTemperature : Real;`.
Score:          0.78  -  Missing units across the model is likely to produce analytical error and invalid comparisons.
Confidence:     HIGH
Impact:         Quantitative analysis, requirement interpretation, and cross-model consistency are at significant risk of unit ambiguity.
Recommendation: Replace bare `Real` with explicit value types carrying units, or annotate all quantitative attributes with units consistently.

ISSUE [COMPLETENESS-016]
Element:        VehicleModel::Vehicle; VehicleModel::Engine; VehicleModel::Transmission; VehicleModel::Wheel; port definitions
Check:          6. Value & Constraint Completeness
Finding:        Critical numerical attributes generally lack explicit min/max/nominal properties in the model elements themselves; bounds are embedded only in separate requirements and not bound to reusable attribute specifications.
Evidence:       Attributes such as `Vehicle.mass`, `Vehicle.maxSpeed`, `Engine.power`, `Transmission.efficiency`, and `Wheel.diameter` are declared only as plain `Real` properties.
Score:          0.49  -  The information exists in requirements, reducing risk, but the values are not structured for direct analytical reuse.
Confidence:     HIGH
Impact:         Parametric analysis and automated checking cannot readily consume bounds as part of the value model.
Recommendation: Introduce value properties/value types with bound metadata or constraint structures directly associated with the attributes.

ISSUE [COMPLETENESS-017]
Element:        VehicleModel::AccelerationAnalysis; VehicleModel::BrakingSafetyAnalysis
Check:          6. Value & Constraint Completeness
Finding:        Constraint models are present but not expressed as reusable `constraint def` constructs bound in a parametric usage context.
Evidence:       The model uses inline `constraint zeroToSixtyEstimate { ... }` and `constraint brakingLoadEstimate { ... }` inside `part def` analyses, but no `constraint def` is declared anywhere.
Score:          0.44  -  Analysis is still partly possible, but reuse and formal parametric binding completeness are limited.
Confidence:     HIGH
Impact:         Constraint reuse, traceability, and consistent quantitative modeling across analyses are reduced.
Recommendation: Define reusable `constraint def` elements and bind them to analysis attributes in explicit parametric contexts.

ISSUE [COMPLETENESS-018]
Element:        Model-Wide
Check:          7. Allocation Completeness
Finding:        No explicit `allocate` relationships are present between functional elements and physical elements.
Evidence:       The model contains actions such as `ProvidePropulsion`, `TransferTorque`, `GenerateRoadTraction`, `DriveVehicle`, etc., and physical parts such as `engine`, `transmission`, and `wheels`, but no `allocate` statements appear anywhere.
Score:          0.72  -  Missing allocation is likely to block architecture traceability and function-to-physical responsibility analysis.
Confidence:     HIGH
Impact:         Engineers cannot trace which physical components realize which functions, or assess completeness of implementation responsibility.
Recommendation: Add `allocate` relationships from functions/actions to the relevant physical parts and ensure traceability in both directions.

ISSUE [COMPLETENESS-019]
Element:        Model-Wide
Check:          8. Scenario & Use Case Coverage
Finding:        Operational mode coverage is incomplete because no explicit scenario models normal idle-only operation, restart/recovery from safe state, or transitions back from degraded operation short of shutdown.
Evidence:       Scenario actions include `NominalDriveMission` and `DegradedDriveMission`; the state machine includes `Idle`, `Driving`, `DegradedOperation`, `SafeState`, `ShuttingDown`, and `Off`, but no scenario covers return/recovery paths or idle-focused operation.
Score:          0.46  -  Coverage is partial and may miss important operating situations, though nominal and degraded missions are at least partly represented.
Confidence:     MEDIUM  -  The criterion asks for all identified modes to be covered, but some omitted scenarios may be intentionally deferred in this demonstrator.
Impact:         Scenario-based validation may miss important transitions and operational corner cases.
Recommendation: Add scenarios covering remaining identified modes and transitions, including idle operation, safe-state recovery if allowed, and degraded-to-shutdown/off alternatives as intended.

ISSUE [COMPLETENESS-020]
Element:        Model-Wide
Check:          9. Verification Methods
Finding:        Most requirements have no defined verification method or linked verification case.
Evidence:       Verification artifacts exist for only a few requirements (`PerformanceVerificationCase`, `SafetyVerificationCase`, `AccelerationTestCase`, `BrakingAnalysisCase`), while many requirements such as `VehicleMassSpecReq`, `VehicleMaxSpeedSpecReq`, `StartupScenarioReq`, and others have no visible verification linkage.
Score:          0.81  -  Missing verification method coverage across most requirements is highly likely to block verification planning and compliance assessment.
Confidence:     HIGH
Impact:         The model cannot support complete requirement verification planning or compliance traceability.
Recommendation: For every requirement, define a verification method and link to one or more verification cases or analyses.

ISSUE [COMPLETENESS-021]
Element:        VehicleModel::PerformanceVerificationCase; VehicleModel::SafetyVerificationCase
Check:          9. Verification Methods
Finding:        The verification definitions use `satisfy` instead of an explicit requirement verification relationship, leaving evidence linkage ambiguous.
Evidence:       `verification def PerformanceVerificationCase { ... satisfy PerformanceReq; }` and similarly for `SafetyVerificationCase`.
Score:          0.57  -  Ambiguous verification linkage can lead to incorrect trace semantics and incomplete evidence mapping.
Confidence:     MEDIUM  -  Exact accepted SysML v2 relation usage may vary by tooling/profile, but the criterion expects explicit verification linkage.
Impact:         It may be unclear whether these elements demonstrate verification evidence or design satisfaction.
Recommendation: Replace or supplement `satisfy` with explicit requirement-to-verification relationships and identify the evidence-producing analysis/test elements.

ISSUE [COMPLETENESS-022]
Element:        VehicleModel::AccelerationTestCase; BrakingAnalysisCase
Check:          9. Verification Methods
Finding:        Verification artifacts at the end of the file are not clearly integrated into the `VehicleModel` package and are not linked to specific requirements.
Evidence:       `verification def AccelerationTestCase { ... subject vehicle : Vehicle; ... }` and `verification def BrakingAnalysisCase { ... }` are declared after the closing brace of `package VehicleModel { ... }`, and neither contains a relation to a requirement.
Score:          0.69  -  Detached and unlinked verification elements can cause traceability breakage and tool interpretation issues.
Confidence:     HIGH
Impact:         Verification coverage accounting may omit these cases or fail to associate them with the intended system and requirements.
Recommendation: Move these verification definitions into the package or import context explicitly, and link each to the requirements it verifies with clear evidence relationships.

Summary Table

| ID | Score | Confidence | One-line description |
|---|---:|---|---|
| COMPLETENESS-001 | 0.68 | HIGH | `PerformanceReq` lacks subject and explicit satisfaction/verification traceability. |
| COMPLETENESS-002 | 0.70 | HIGH | `SafetyReq` lacks subject and explicit satisfaction/verification traceability. |
| COMPLETENESS-003 | 0.52 | HIGH | No derived/refinement links connect stakeholder requirements to detailed requirements. |
| COMPLETENESS-004 | 0.55 | MEDIUM | Non-functional requirement coverage is incomplete and only partially measurable. |
| COMPLETENESS-005 | 0.63 | HIGH | `myCar` does not specialize or assign values to its four wheel parts. |
| COMPLETENESS-006 | 0.41 | MEDIUM | `Wheel` is under-specified for likely analysis and integration needs. |
| COMPLETENESS-007 | 0.30 | HIGH | `externalLoad` is an orphaned fragment not integrated into any composition or context. |
| COMPLETENESS-008 | 0.74 | HIGH | Vehicle boundary port `powerOut` is unconnected internally and externally. |
| COMPLETENESS-009 | 0.58 | HIGH | Port/interface definitions lack explicit directional flow semantics. |
| COMPLETENESS-010 | 0.88 | HIGH | `DriveCarFlow` references undefined or wrong-kind behavioral elements (`StartVehicle`/`StopVehicle`). |
| COMPLETENESS-011 | 0.67 | HIGH | Core actions are stubs with no typed interfaces or internal behavior. |
| COMPLETENESS-012 | 0.66 | HIGH | State transitions have no triggers, guards, or effects. |
| COMPLETENESS-013 | 0.61 | HIGH | `OperatingModes` lacks an explicit initial state/pseudostate. |
| COMPLETENESS-014 | 0.39 | MEDIUM | Operational states lack entry/exit/do behavior where behavior is likely expected. |
| COMPLETENESS-015 | 0.78 | HIGH | Quantitative attributes are bare `Real` values without explicit units. |
| COMPLETENESS-016 | 0.49 | HIGH | Critical attributes lack structured min/max/nominal value modeling on the elements themselves. |
| COMPLETENESS-017 | 0.44 | HIGH | Constraints are inline only and not modeled as reusable `constraint def` parametrics. |
| COMPLETENESS-018 | 0.72 | HIGH | No explicit allocations connect functions to physical elements. |
| COMPLETENESS-019 | 0.46 | MEDIUM | Scenario coverage does not span all identified modes/transitions. |
| COMPLETENESS-020 | 0.81 | HIGH | Most requirements lack defined verification methods and linked verification cases. |
| COMPLETENESS-021 | 0.57 | MEDIUM | Verification definitions use ambiguous `satisfy` linkage instead of explicit verification relation. |
| COMPLETENESS-022 | 0.69 | HIGH | End-of-file verification cases are detached from the package and unlinked to requirements. |

Overall Assessment Score: 0.41

Model Status for this Dimension: Critical deficiencies - major rework required before this model can be used.
